title: A Little Business
developmental_frame: At 9–10, children can make a simple offer to a few real people and keep a simple tally, though real profit and complex pricing are still beyond them. The leap this quarter is doing it *more than once* and *keeping track* — discovering that repeating and counting teaches you what works. Keep money tiny and the whole thing playful. This sets up the year-end capstone where several skills come together.
authentic_value_meaning: Value here is making something a few real people genuinely want, and using a simple tally to see what worked. It's the first real 'little business' — not about getting rich, but about the loop of offer, count, learn, improve.

motivation_lever.mechanism: The child runs a tiny real business — a few offers to a few real people — and keeps score. Real people choosing (or not), and a tally that visibly adds up, makes it feel like a genuine venture rather than pretend. Watching the count grow and spotting what people liked is motivating in the same way keeping score in a game is.

artifact: A small repeated offer made to at least three real people, with a simple tally of yeses/nos and what was made or earned, plus one noticed pattern about what people liked.
success_criteria: The child offered the same thing to at least three real people.
They kept a simple written tally of the results.
They can name one thing people liked or one thing they'd change, based on the tally.

lessons.title: Do it more than once
lessons.concept: In Q1 you made one offer. A little business means doing it a few times with a few people. Repeating is where you start to learn — one yes could be luck, but three tries shows you a real pattern.
lessons.real_example: Selling bookmarks to one aunt tells you little. Offering them to three neighbours tells you if people actually want bookmarks — or if you should make something else.
lessons.apply_task: Pick something simple to offer (make it, or a small service) and list three real people you could offer it to.
lessons.reflection_prompt: Why is trying three people better than trying one? What might three answers show you that one can't?
lessons.feedback_task: Run your idea past a parent and check your three people are ones you can safely reach.
lessons.teach_back_task: Explain to a sibling why doing a thing three times teaches more than doing it once.
lessons.ai_judgment_note: The real people you'll offer to are from your own life — a computer can't pick them. That's yours to plan.
success_criteria: An offer and three real people to approach are chosen.

lessons.title: Keep a simple tally
lessons.concept: A little business keeps score. Make a simple tally: who you asked, yes or no, and what you made or earned. The tally turns your business from guessing into knowing.
lessons.real_example: A tally might read: Aunt — yes — RM2; Neighbour — no; Friend — yes — RM2. Now you can *see* it worked twice out of three, and earned RM4. Real numbers, not vibes.
lessons.apply_task: Make a tally sheet with columns: who, yes/no, what I made. Get it ready before you start offering.
lessons.reflection_prompt: Why write it down instead of just remembering? What might you forget without a tally?
lessons.feedback_task: Show your blank tally to an adult and check it's clear and easy to fill in.
lessons.teach_back_task: Show a friend how a simple tally helps a little business keep score.
lessons.ai_judgment_note: Keep your tally by hand — writing down your own real results helps you understand them. This is your score to keep.
success_criteria: A clear tally sheet is ready to use.

lessons.title: Run it and notice what people liked
lessons.concept: Now make your offers and fill in your tally. As you go, notice *why* people said yes or no — 'she liked the colour', 'he didn't need one'. Those little reasons are the most valuable part of the tally.
lessons.real_example: You notice both yeses loved the sparkly bookmarks but nobody wanted the plain ones. That's gold — it tells you exactly what to make more of.
lessons.apply_task: Make your three offers, fill in your tally, and jot the reason next to each yes or no.
lessons.reflection_prompt: Do you see a pattern in the reasons? Patterns are the business telling you what to do next.
lessons.feedback_task: Share your filled tally with a parent and talk about what the reasons show.
lessons.teach_back_task: Tell someone one pattern you spotted in why people said yes or no.
lessons.ai_judgment_note: The reasons come from real people you spoke to — no computer could tell you why *your* neighbour said yes. That listening is the valuable skill.
success_criteria: At least three real offers were made and tallied with reasons.
The child spotted at least one pattern.

lessons.title: Do one thing better
lessons.concept: The point of a tally is to improve. Look at your pattern and pick one thing to do better next time — make more of what people liked, change what they didn't. That's how a little business grows.
lessons.real_example: Your tally says sparkly sells, plain doesn't. Next time: make mostly sparkly ones. One clear improvement, straight from your own numbers.
lessons.apply_task: Based on your tally, write down the one thing you'll do better next time and why.
lessons.reflection_prompt: How did it feel to change your plan based on real results instead of guessing? That's what real businesses do.
lessons.feedback_task: Tell a parent or mentor your improvement idea and why your tally points to it.
lessons.teach_back_task: Teach someone the loop: offer, tally, spot the pattern, do one thing better.
lessons.ai_judgment_note: Your improvement comes from your own real results — that's the honest way to get better. Trust your tally over any guess.
success_criteria: One specific improvement is chosen, justified by the tally.
The child can explain the offer-tally-improve loop.
